ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general coffee beverage class; no specific coconut latte subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers prepared coffee beverages broadly; no coconut-milk-specific coffee code exists. Approximate match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for coconut latte; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard café serving of coconut latte is approximately 240 ml (240 g), equivalent to a regular-sized cup containing one or two shots of espresso topped with steamed coconut milk.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut Latte — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Coconut Latte (cr_id G00010730) with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for milk-based coffee beverage
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for coconut latte in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the closest analogous approved-source entry: latte with reduced-fat milk (GI ~30–40, University of Sydney GI Database). Coconut milk has a similarly low GI due to its fat and protein content blunting glucose response. Glycemic load per 240 g serving calculated as: (35 × 4.35 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.4) / 100 = 3.7 g. This is a low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (35 mg/100 g) with beneficial potassium (152 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Coconut latte is suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed unsweetened; its very low sodium content and moderate potassium level support blood pressure management, but sweetened or flavoured syrup versions should be avoided as added sugar can elevate cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.35 g/100 g), low sugar (4.06 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~35)
type2_diabetes.guidance: An unsweetened coconut latte has a low carbohydrate load and estimated low glycaemic index, making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, any added sugar, syrups, or sweetened coconut milk will substantially increase the glycaemic impact and should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~1.2 g/100 g); cholesterol 7 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the saturated fat per 100 g is modest in this diluted beverage, coconut milk is a source of medium-chain saturated fatty acids; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to one serving per day and request unsweetened preparation, as regular consumption of coconut-fat-containing beverages may contribute to LDL elevation over time.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chlorogenic acid polyphenols from espresso; low saturated fat in diluted form
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Espresso in the coconut latte provides chlorogenic acid polyphenols with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties; the overall saturated fat content at 1.2 g/100 g is well below the 5 g/100 g threshold, making unsweetened coconut latte acceptable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis in moderate frequency.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coconut latte is a café beverage made by combining one or two shots of espresso with steamed or frothed coconut milk, served hot or iced. It is a dairy-free alternative to the traditional cow's-milk latte, popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asian café culture. The flavour profile is mildly sweet and nutty from the coconut milk, balanced by the bold bitterness of espresso. The texture is smooth and creamy, lighter than a full-fat dairy latte. Per 100 g, the HPB Singapore Foodo database records 43 kcal, 2.81 g protein, 1.61 g fat, 4.35 g carbohydrate, 4.06 g sugar, 35 mg sodium, 152 mg potassium, and 105 mg calcium. The beverage is naturally dairy-free, gluten-free, and suitable for vegan and halal diets. Its low carbohydrate content and estimated low glycaemic index (~35) make it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed unsweetened. The potassium content provides modest cardiovascular benefit. Coconut milk contributes medium-chain triglycerides and a small amount of saturated fat; in the diluted beverage form, saturated fat is approximately 1.2 g/100 g, which is well below clinical concern thresholds. Espresso contributes chlorogenic acid polyphenols with anti-inflammatory properties relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Coconut latte is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a mid-morning snack in Singapore café settings.
common_user_queries: Is coconut latte suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in a coconut latte?
Is coconut latte good for high blood pressure?
Coconut latte vs oat milk latte calories
Can I drink coconut latte if I have high cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Coconut Latte (cr_id G00010730) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source for this beverage preparation; this is plausible as coconut milk used in café lattes is typically strained and filtered, removing most fibre. saturated_fat_g of 1.20 g/100 g is estimated from the total fat of 1.61 g/100 g based on the known fatty acid profile of diluted coconut milk (approximately 75% of fat as saturated); this is an internal estimation as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this beverage. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for Coconut Latte and no approved secondary source was used to supplement them for this beverage. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for milk-based latte; no direct GI measurement for coconut latte exists in the approved GI database. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code for coconut latte exists as of 2026.
